content_en:
Al-Muqaddimah is not only a work of political theory and historical philosophy but also a remarkable survey of Islamic intellectual culture, educational methods, and economic life in the medieval period. These sections, which occupy substantial portions of the text, have proven as valuable for historians of Islamic civilization as the famous political theory.

Ibn Khaldun's survey of the Islamic sciences — theology, jurisprudence, hadith, Quranic sciences, philosophy, mathematics, astronomy, medicine — provides a comprehensive map of the intellectual landscape of the medieval Islamic world as seen by an encyclopedically educated fourteenth-century Tunisian scholar. He describes each discipline's subject matter, its major works and figures, its relationship to the other disciplines, and its position within the educational curriculum.

His discussion of Islamic education is particularly valuable for historians of pedagogy. Ibn Khaldun analyzed the methods by which Islamic scholarship was transmitted, the role of memorization, the function of the teacher-student relationship, the organization of the madrasa curriculum, and the differences between educational traditions in different regions of the Islamic world. He offered pointed criticisms of pedagogical methods he considered counterproductive — excessive harshness toward students, emphasis on memorization over understanding — that anticipate modern educational debates.

On economics, Ibn Khaldun anticipated several insights that later economists would reach independently. He described the labor theory of value — that the value of goods reflects the human labor involved in their production — and analyzed the relationship between prices, supply, and demand. His discussion of the effects of taxation on economic activity, arguing that excessive taxation depresses production and ultimately reduces state revenues, has been cited by modern commentators as an early statement of what is now sometimes called the Laffer curve.

His account of the role of craft skills in civilizational development, the way that cities generate specialized production, and the relationship between population density and economic sophistication reflects both personal observation and theoretical insight.
